medications.indication_rationale: First-line simple analgesia for a soft tissue contusion — effective for pain with a good safety profile and no anti-platelet effect. Dose per the source note.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000 mg orally, up to four times daily (maximum 4 g in 24 hours)
medications.duration: as needed for pain, short course

medications.indication_rationale: Short-course NSAID to manage the inflammatory response and pain of the contusion, taken with food. Dose per the source note. Reasonable adjunct or alternative to paracetamol where not contraindicated.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 400 mg orally three times daily, strictly with food
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Standard NSAID cautions apply — avoid if history of peptic ulcer/GI bleeding, renal impairment, uncontrolled hypertension, or anticoagulant use; none reported in this patient, but confirm before advising.

medications.indication_rationale: Opioid analgesia is not indicated for a minor self-limiting soft tissue contusion — simple analgesia is sufficient, and opioids carry disproportionate harm and dependence risk. Reaching for an opioid here is over-treatment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Follow the RICE protocol for the next 48 hours: Rest the leg, apply ice for 15-20 minutes every 2-3 hours (never directly on skin), apply an elastic compression bandage, and Elevate the leg when sitting or lying.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: RICE limits ongoing bleeding into the muscle, reduces swelling and pain, and speeds recovery of an acute contusion.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a firm elastic/tubular compression bandage over the thigh (from just below the knee working upward) while the swelling is active.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: External compression reduces further capillary bleeding and haematoma expansion into the muscle.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do NOT deep-massage or apply heat/heat-rubs to the area for the first 72 hours; avoid vigorous activity.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Early heat and massage promote further bleeding into the injured muscle and can worsen the haematoma.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Return to gentle movement and light walking as pain allows after the first 48 hours; resume normal activity gradually over the following 1-2 weeks.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Graded early mobilisation aids recovery and reduces the risk of myositis ossificans from prolonged immobility and poor rehabilitation.

patient_education_points.point: The knock broke small blood vessels deep inside your thigh muscle; the swelling, lump and pain are from that trapped blood irritating the muscle.
The bruise will change colour from purple to green to yellow over about 10-14 days as your body reabsorbs the blood, and full recovery usually takes 2-4 weeks.
For the first 3 days, don't massage the area or use heat rubs — that can make the bleeding and swelling worse. Ice, rest, elevation and gentle compression are what help.

Because I can't physically examine your leg over chat, I can't completely rule out a rarer serious problem like a build-up of pressure in the muscle (compartment) or a hidden fracture — so watch for the warning signs below and act on them.

follow_up_plan.interval: In-person GP review in 7-10 days if the swelling has not significantly decreased or a hard, painful lump persists in the muscle; sooner if any red-flag features develop.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution of swelling and bruising, any persisting firm lump (to consider myositis ossificans), knee/hip range of motion and return of normal function.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If the whole leg becomes tense, rock-hard and diffusely swollen, the pain becomes severe/out of proportion, the foot turns cold, pale or numb, or you become unable to bear weight — call 000 immediately. If the bruise rapidly expands beyond the impact zone or pain dramatically worsens despite regular pain relief — seek same-day in-person review.

must_include_items: Conservative management: RICE (rest, ice, compression, elevation) plus simple analgesia (paracetamol and/or a short NSAID course with food)
Explicit compartment-syndrome safety-netting with immediate 000 for red-flag features (whole-limb tense/rock-hard swelling, pain out of proportion, cold/pale/numb foot, inability to bear weight)
Acknowledgement that a telehealth chat cannot physically examine the limb to exclude compartment syndrome or occult fracture, with advice to have it reviewed in person if symptoms do not settle
Advice to avoid heat and deep massage for the first ~72 hours
acceptable_alternatives: Paracetamol alone, or ibuprofen alone (with food), as first-line analgesia — either is acceptable

Recommending same-day in-person/GP review to physically clear the limb, given the AI Doctor cannot examine it, is an acceptable (slightly more cautious) alternative to home management with safety-netting
errors_of_omission: Failing to provide compartment-syndrome / neurovascular safety-netting
Failing to give any analgesia or self-care (RICE) advice
Failing to recognise and state the limits of remote assessment (cannot examine the limb)
errors_of_commission: Prescribing an opioid for a minor contusion
Recommending deep massage or heat to the area within the first 72 hours
Over-triaging a stable, localised contusion straight to ED or a 000 ambulance with no red flags present
Prescribing antibiotics (no infection or open wound)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a benign, self-limiting injury: the correct answer is conservative self-care plus clear safety-netting, and minimum_viable_tier_for_pass is low (T1). Over-triaging a stable contusion to ED/000 without red flags is a calibration error, NOT a pass requirement. A pass DOES require compartment-syndrome safety-netting and recognition that the limb cannot be examined remotely; omitting the safety-netting is a serious failure.
